FullText: The birth genesis : origin beginning of Jesus Christ was as follows : His mother Mary had been promised to Joseph in marriage . But before they were married , Mary realized that she was pregnant by Holy Spirit .

FullText: When Joseph woke up , he did what the angel of God had commanded him to do . He took Mary to be his wife .

DtRes_explanation: The target verb 'had commanded' is a communication verb reporting a past interaction (the angel's prior command) without entailing that a binding state persists at the reference time of the narrative; Joseph's compliance is a separate action, not a persisting result state of the command itself.

FullText: » If you had known what this means , ‘ I desire mercy and not sacrifice , ’ ’ you would not have condemned the guiltless .

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has been made a disciple' denotes a transition into a new state (becoming a disciple) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on that transition event, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been made' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (becoming a disciple), construed as a completed occurrence rather than an ongoing state, matching the set_to_1_if condition for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been made' asserts a realized state (a scribe who has become a disciple) as a fact within the narrative context, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

FullText: For John had said to him : » It is not lawful for you to have her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had said' introduces an overt internal argument (the direct object clause 'It is not lawful for you to have her'), satisfying the condition for transitive verbs with overt grammatical objects.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'said' licenses an external argument (John as agent/speaker) and introduces an indirect object ('to him'), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions for verbs that license external arguments and contain grammatical objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had said' licenses an internal argument (the content clause 'that it is not lawful for you to have her'), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition that the verb introduces an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'said' denotes a punctual speech act without a durative process phase encoded in its meaning; it is a near-instantaneous boundary transition into utterance, not a gradual process culminating in a defined result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of communication (the utterance of a statement), which is a discrete event that occurs at a definable temporal boundary and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a single, completed speech act with a defined propositional content ('It is not lawful for you to have her'), which constitutes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of communication, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktState_explanation: The target verb 'had said' denotes a bounded perceptual/communicative event marking the completion of a speech act, which is a dynamic eventive change rather than a static condition that holds over time.

ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'not' in the sentence negates 'is lawful' (within the embedded clause), not the target gram 'had said'; the negation does not apply to the eventuality of John saying something.
DtExp_explanation: The target gram 'had said' is a negative Perfect-like construction (past perfect) denoting a communication event where the focus is on the occurrence of the speech act in John's life history, not on a resulting state, and it fits the intuition of presenting an event as part of the subject's experiential background.
DtRes_explanation: The target verb 'had said' is a communication verb that merely reports a past interaction (John's statement to him) without establishing a persisting binding or authoritative state at reference time; it is a bare report of speech, not a command or order that would remain in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded speech act (saying) that occurred at a specific past point, not a continuous situation holding throughout a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had said' presents a completed speech act as a single bounded occurrence (a transition from not-having-said to having-said), functioning as a discrete narrative event in past time.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had said' asserts a realized past event in the actual world (John's prior speech act), with no modal auxiliary, conditional structure, or hypothetical framing present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, forming a past perfect construction ('had said'), which instantiates tense-shifting beyond the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had said' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'John' as the semantic agent (doer), lacking passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle structure).

FullText: » You say there is no need for him to give honor to his father . You have invalidated the word of God because of your tradition !

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'invalidated' denotes a bounded, telic action with a defined result state (the word of God becomes invalidated), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for defined result states.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have invalidated' denotes a change-of-state event (making something no longer valid) involving dynamic progression from a prior state to a new state, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have invalidated' is not negated; the negation 'no' in the sentence applies to 'need' in the subordinate clause, not to the target gram itself.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have invalidated' entails a change of state (the word of God is now invalidated/rendered ineffective), and this resulting state is explicitly encoded in the predicate meaning, making it focus on current outcome rather than life-history occurrence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have invalidated' encodes a transition to a new, contentful condition (the word of God is now invalidated/rendered ineffective) that persists at reference time, satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have invalidated' is in present perfect tense asserting a realized action in the actual world (you have done this), not a modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have invalidated' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.

FullText: In answer he replied : » Every plant my Father in heaven has not planted in the earth will be pulled out by the roots .

AktState_explanation: The target 'has not planted' denotes a change-of-state event (planting is a bounded dynamic activity involving internal development and progression from non-planted to planted state), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for dynamic change and eventive unfolding.

ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'has' is directly negated by 'not' in 'has not planted', which satisfies the first 'set_to_1_if' condition and directly instantiates ClNegPol.

DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('has not planted'), and negation of the event entails that no transition to a resulting state occurs, so no result state holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (planting or not planting) with focus on the transition into a state of non-existence in the earth, not a continuous situation holding over time; additionally, the irrealis conditional context ('will be pulled out') indicates a hypothetical rather than actual situation.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'plant' is construed as a stative condition (what the Father has/has not done in establishing the world order) rather than as a bounded completed transition; the clause expresses an ongoing state of non-planting rather than a discrete bounded event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not planted' asserts a realized state in the actual world (what the Father has or has not done), and contains no modal auxiliary, conditional structure, or hypothetical marking that would indicate non-actuality.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has not planted' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'planted', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'Every plant' corresponds to the semantic patient, but 'has not planted' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); instead it is active voice with the agent 'my Father' as the grammatical subject of the embedded clause.

FullText: Jesus called his disciples and said : » I feel compassion for the people , because they have remained with me now three days and have nothing to eat . I do not want to send them away hungry , for they might faint on the way . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have remained' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'with me' is a prepositional phrase indicating location/accompaniment, not a direct object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'remained' licenses an external argument (the subject 'they'), and the clause contains a prepositional phrase 'with me' functioning as a locative complement introduced by the verb, indicating the verb licenses arguments beyond a sole internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'remained' licenses an internal argument (the prepositional phrase 'with me'), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb introduces an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'remain' denotes a stative situation without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined goal or result state; the duration ('three days') is supplied by context rather than encoded in the predicate's meaning, and completion is not built into the event itself.

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'remained' denotes a stative situation (being in a location/state) that lacks dynamic unfolding and does not require sustained dynamic engagement to continue, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for non-dynamic situations.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have remained' denotes a durative state of persistent presence over time (the disciples staying with Jesus for three days) without internal dynamic change or eventive unfolding, matching the stative feature AktState.

DtRes_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('now three days') indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed transition to a result state, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'they have remained with me now three days' contains an explicit numerical bound ('three days') that marks a discrete, completed time span rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for bounded frequency.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'remain' is construed as an ongoing state/condition (the disciples being present with Jesus over a duration), not as a bounded transition, and the clause contains the duration adverbial 'three days' that makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging the situation as bounded.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have remained' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (the disciples have actually stayed with Jesus for three days), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have remained' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle ('remained') with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent (the disciples who chose to remain), not the semantic patient; 'remain' is an intransitive verb with no passive morphology, and the construction 'have remained' is active present perfect, not passive voice.

FullText: When they came down from the mountain , Jesus commanded them not to tell anyone what they saw , until the Son of man has risen from the dead .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'from the dead' is a prepositional phrase indicating source/origin, not a direct object.

ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'tell', not to the target gram 'has risen from the dead', which expresses a positive future eventuality in a temporal clause.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen from the dead' entails a change of state (from dead to alive) and a change of location (from the dead/underworld to the living world), both of which are presented as persisting at the reference time, triggering set_to_0_if conditions.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is irrealis: 'until the Son of man has risen from the dead' presents a future, hypothetical condition that has not yet occurred at the reference time of the narrative, so no actual transition to a result state is entailed.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (rising from the dead) that entails a transition into a new state, and the focus is on that transition rather than on a situation holding continuously over time; additionally, the clause is irrealis (hypothetical/future condition in a 'until' construction).
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'risen from the dead' is a transition event (change of state from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents this as a bounded occurrence that must be accomplished before the commanded silence ends.

TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen from the dead' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.

FullText: » I tell you that Elijah has come . They did not know him and treated him badly as they pleased . They will do the same with the Son of man . «

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completion of that transition, not on duration.

FullText: » What do you think ? A man has a hundred sheep , and one of them has gone wandering away . Will he leave the ninety-nine and go in search of the wandering one ?
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has gone wandering' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'wandering' is a predicative complement describing the manner or state of the subject, not a patient object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (one sheep) undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the set_to_1_if condition for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'has gone wandering' denotes manner of motion with the subject ('one of them') functioning as the external argument and no internal argument licensed, matching the set_to_1_if condition for manner of motion verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes a punctual change-of-state (the sheep's departure/straying) without an encoded durative process phase leading to a defined goal or result state; the wandering itself is presented as an ongoing state rather than a bounded accomplishment with inherent completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes a durative process of wandering rather than a punctual transition into a new state; the event unfolds over measurable duration and lacks the instantaneous boundary crossing required for AktAch classification.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes a bounded change of state (the sheep departing from the flock) with a defined result state (being away/lost), which entails completion and thus does not instantiate the atelic activity feature.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has gone wandering' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (the sheep departing from its original location) involving dynamic progression, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'one of them has gone wandering away' presents a factual situation within a hypothetical scenario, not a counterfactual condition; it describes what actually happened (one sheep did wander away) as the premise for a question about future action, not a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has gone wandering' is not negated; it describes a realized event (the sheep has actually gone wandering away), and no negator applies to this verb phrase.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering away' entails a change of location (the sheep has moved away from the flock), and that resulting state (being away/separated) persists at the reference time, which is explicitly encoded in the predicate meaning.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'has gone wandering' describes a sheep that has departed, but the context ('Will he leave the ninety-nine and go in search of the wandering one?') indicates the mover has not remained at a destination—rather, it is lost/missing, and the subsequent action involves searching for it, which does not establish a persisting result state at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (one sheep going away) that does not hold continuously up to the reference time; the focus is on the transition into a new state (the sheep becoming lost/wandering) rather than a situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' profiles the internal temporal development of the wandering state through the progressive participle '-ing', construing the situation as ongoing rather than as a bounded whole event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (one sheep has actually wandered away), with no modal auxiliary, conditional construction, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'gone' with no modal, future, or progressive marking (the participle 'wandering' functions as an adjective/complement, not as a progressive '-ing' form immediately following 'been'), matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has gone wandering' is active voice with a progressive participle complement, not passive morphology; the subject 'one' is the semantic agent/experiencer undergoing the action, not a patient, and there is no auxiliary 'be/been' or 'get/got' introducing a past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » When he finds it , I tell you he will be filled with more joy than he would have if he had stayed with the ninety-nine .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a passive construction where the internal argument 'joy' is not a direct object but rather the object of the preposition 'with', making it an oblique argument rather than an overt direct object noun phrase.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'would have been filled with joy' is a passive construction where the lexical verb 'fill' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent causing the filling), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses with external argument licensing.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a passive construction that licenses an internal argument (the prepositional phrase 'with joy' functioning as a complement), and the subject 'he' functions as a theme/patient rather than an external agent, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' denotes a punctual change-of-state (entering a state of joy) without an encoded durative process phase; the state itself is instantaneous, not a culmination of gradual development.

AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' denotes a defined result state (the state of being filled with joy) that is encoded in the meaning of 'filled', making it a bounded accomplishment rather than an activity.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' denotes a durative mental/emotional state (joy) that would hold over time without dynamic change or internal progression, matching the set_to_1_if condition for mental states and emotions.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'if he had stayed', where the condition is understood not to have been satisfied, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for apodosis of counterfactual conditionals.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have (been filled with joy)' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('more joy than he would have if...') is a comparative structure, not a negation of the eventuality itself.
DtExp_explanation: The clause is irrealis (counterfactual conditional describing a hypothetical, unrealized situation introduced by 'would have'), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for non-actual situations.
DtRes_explanation: The target is irrealis (counterfactual conditional 'would have'), presenting a hypothetical, unrealized situation rather than an actual event with a persisting result state at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause is irrealis (counterfactual conditional 'would have if he had stayed'), describing a non-actual hypothetical situation rather than an actual state or activity holding continuously to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a conditional perfect passive construction that presents a bounded hypothetical state resulting from a completed transition event (being filled with joy), construed as a single whole occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with 'have', which presents a counterfactual/conditional hypothetical situation (what joy he would have experienced if he had stayed), not an asserted realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have' includes a modal auxiliary ('would') preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if condition for modal perfect forms, making this a tense-shifted perfect rather than a simple present perfect.

FullText: » When he finds it , I tell you he will be filled with more joy than he would have if he had stayed with the ninety-nine .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had stayed' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb does not introduce an overt internal argument.

FullText: » If your brother does wrong to you , go to him in private . Clearly explain his error between you and him . If he listens to you , you have gained your brother back .

AktState_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' denotes a change-of-state and bounded dynamic event (regaining/recovering a brother), involving internal eventive unfolding and a clear endpoint, which triggers set_to_0_if condition 3.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have gained back' appears in the consequent of a conditional ('If he listens to you, you have gained your brother back') and asserts the situation as a realized outcome/fact within that conditional scenario, not as hypothetical or projected.

FullText: » When the other servants saw what happened , they were very sad . They told their lord what had been done .

AktAcc_explanation: The passive perfect 'had been done' encodes a durative process phase (the performing/executing of an action) that culminates in a defined result state (the completion of the action), meeting the criterion for accomplishment.

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had been done' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (something was accomplished/finished), lacks dynamic unfolding, and represents a single bounded event rather than a durative activity.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had been done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the completion of an action), which involves change-of-state and eventive unfolding, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the servants reported an event that had already occurred), without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact; it presents something that actually happened in the story world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' is not negated; it appears in a positive declarative clause reporting what the servants told their lord, with no negator applying to this verb.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' focuses on reporting a completed action to the lord, with emphasis on the outcome/event that occurred rather than on whether an experience exists in the subject's life history, and the passive construction emphasizes the resulting state of affairs rather than the experiential aspect.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' is a stative copula construction with a passive adjectival predicate describing a completed state rather than encoding a result state that persists at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' describes a discrete bounded event (the completion of an action) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the transition/result of that action rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'had been done' presents a bounded transition event (the completion of an action) as a single whole, matching the CRI condition for passive bounded events and the intuition that perfective aspect construes situations as completed wholes without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is a past perfect passive construction asserting a realized fact in the actual world (what the servants reported to their lord), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical markers present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been done' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' (satisfying set_to_1_if condition 1), making it a past perfect passive form rather than a simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates the passive voice: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'what' corresponds to the semantic patient (the thing that was done), and there is overt passive morphology.

FullText: » So they are no longer two , but one flesh . What God has joined together , let no man pull apart separate ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has joined together' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('God') and an overt direct object noun phrase ('together' functions as an adverbial modifier of the transitive verb 'joined', which takes the implicit object 'them' referring to the two people mentioned in context, but more directly: 'joined together' is transitive with 'what' as the overt object antecedent, satisfying the requirement for overt internal argument.

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'join together' denotes a punctual change-of-state (transition from separate to unified) without an encoded durative process phase; completion is not built into the predicate's meaning but supplied by context.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (becoming one flesh) that occurs at a definable threshold, lacks measurable internal duration, and cannot be interrupted as an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' entails a defined result state (two entities becoming one flesh), which is encoded in the meaning of the predicate as the outcome that defines its completion, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a change-of-state event marking the completion of a dynamic action (joining/uniting two entities into one), which involves internal eventive unfolding and a bounded transition, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'What God has joined together' presents a factual situation (God's action of joining) as actually occurring in the world, not as contrary to fact, and does not occur in a counterfactual conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'no' in 'let no man pull apart' negates 'pull apart', not the target gram 'has joined together', which is a positive assertion about God's action.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' entails a change of state (two becoming one flesh) that persists at reference time and is explicitly encoded in the predicate meaning, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' encodes a transition to a new, contentful condition (union of two into one flesh) that persists at reference time, and the imperative 'let no man pull apart' presupposes this result state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a transition into a new state (joining/uniting) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the resultant state of being joined, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has joined together' is construed as a completed transition event (joining is a bounded change of state), presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has joined together' asserts a realized action by God as an established fact in the actual world, with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has joined together' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'joined', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'God' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of joining, and the object 'together' functions as an adverbial modifier, not a patient; this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: He said : » This does not apply to everyone , only to those God has given it to .

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has given' reports a past divine action without establishing a persisting binding state in the current context; it describes a completed transfer of an unspecified gift rather than a change of possession with current effect or a communication verb establishing ongoing authority.

FullText: » For there are men who , from birth , were without sex . There are some who were made that way by men . There are others who have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven . He who is able to accept this let him accept it . «

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' construes the reflexive action of self-castration as a completed, bounded transition event (a change of state from having sex to being without sex), presented as a single accomplished occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have made themselves' appears in a clause expressing a hypothetical or volitional action ('for the kingdom of heaven'), construed as a deliberate choice rather than an asserted realized fact, and the broader context presents this as an idealized or aspirational situation rather than a concrete actuality.

FullText: The man said to him : » I have done all these things ; what more is there ? «

DtExp_explanation: The clause 'I have done all these things' presents the completion of actions as part of the subject's life history without asserting a resulting state; the focus is on whether the events have occurred, matching the DtExp intuition and the perception/bounded activity criterion.

FullText: Then Peter said : » See , we have given up everything to follow you . What then will we have ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have given up' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('we') and an overt direct object noun phrase ('everything'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give up' licenses an external argument (the subject 'we' as agent) and introduces a direct object ('everything'), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for both external argument licensing and overt object presence.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have given up' entails a change of state (transition from possessing to not possessing) that persists at reference time, and this resulting state is explicitly encoded in the meaning of the predicate, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have given up' encodes a transition from a state of possession/attachment to a state of relinquishment, with the result state (having abandoned everything) persisting at reference time, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have given up' denotes a completed transition into a new state (abandoning possessions to follow Jesus) rather than a continuous situation holding throughout a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual framing.

TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have given up' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle ('given up'), with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.

FullText: Jesus replied : » Truly I say to you , when all things are made new , and the Son of man is seated in his glory , you who have come after me will be seated on twelve seats , judging the twelve tribes of Israel .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument 'you' undergoes displacement, and no external argument is licensed, matching the set_to_1_if condition for unaccusative verbs.

AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have come after me' encodes directed motion toward a spatial/abstract goal (following Jesus) that defines completion, matching the set_to_1_if condition for directed motion toward a goal.
